content.text: Element Variables

An element variable is a regular JavaScript variable that holds a JSX element. You compute which element to render before the return statement and then refer to that variable inside the JSX. The pattern separates the decision logic from the structure of the rendered output — making both easier to read and easier to extend.

attrs.alt: Two panels comparing inline JSX conditional (ternary nested in JSX) versus element variable approach (if/else before return, clean JSX reference)
attrs.title: Element Variables vs Inline JSX

content.text: The Core Pattern
Any JSX expression can be stored in a variable. The variable behaves like any other value — you can conditionally assign it, pass it to functions, or interpolate it with
in the return block:
The return block now reads like a layout — Logo, Nav, and the auth control, whatever it happens to be. The
if/else
statement above it handles all the decision logic without competing with the structure for attention.
When to Use Element Variables
Element variables add value when inline conditions make the JSX hard to read. The key signals:
The conditional branch is a multi-line element or wraps several props.
You have more than two branches (ternary stops working cleanly).
The same element appears in multiple places in the return block.
Inline conditions nest more than one level deep.
When the branch is a single short element and the condition is obvious, inline ternary or
is fine. Element variables solve a readability problem, not every conditional.
Multiple Branches with if/else if
Element variables are the natural fit for three or more branches. Ternaries collapse readability past two branches;
if/else if
scales cleanly:

Compare this to the inline version — four nested ternaries in JSX — and the advantage becomes obvious. The return block is constant: always a section with a header and content. Only the content variable changes.
Using const Instead of let
If all branches can be covered by a ternary (two branches), prefer
const
to signal that the variable is assigned exactly once:
Use
let
only when the variable genuinely needs reassignment.
const
communicates "decided once, used once."
Reusing the Same Element Multiple Times

Another good reason to hoist an element into a variable: you need it in two places in the same render. Inline expressions cannot be shared without repetition or helper functions, but a variable can be placed anywhere:
React will render the same element reference in both places. The decision is made once, and the result is reused without duplication.
Element Variables with Props Computed Separately
The technique extends naturally to cases where props themselves need computation. Derive all values before the return statement:

The return block is a clean template. All business logic — price calculation, discount, badge selection — lives above it. Adding a new badge type or a new pricing rule only touches the logic section, not the template.
Element Variables vs Extracted Components
Both element variables and helper components separate decision logic from JSX. Choose based on scope:
Approach
Shares state/scope?
Reusable elsewhere?
Use when
Element variable
Yes (same function scope)
No (local)
Simplifying one component's JSX
Extracted component
No (receives props)
Yes
Used in multiple places or complex enough to test alone

Element variables are a lightweight refactor that does not require moving code to a new file or threading props through an extra layer. Reach for them first; extract to a component when the element variable grows too large or needs to be shared across files.
Null as a Valid Element Variable Value
Element variables can hold
null
explicitly. This is intentional:
null
in JSX renders nothing, so the element variable pattern works cleanly for optional UI:

Each element variable clearly documents what the button is and when it appears. Adding a new permission requires one new variable and one line in the return block — no hunting through nested ternaries.
TypeScript: Typing Element Variables
TypeScript infers
JSX.Element | null
for ternary-assigned element variables automatically. For
let
declarations used with
if/else
, annotate the type explicitly so the compiler knows what assignments are valid:
Common Patterns in Real Codebases
Element variables appear under several common names. Recognising these patterns helps you read and collaborate on existing React codebases:
Testing Components That Use Element Variables

Element variables are an internal implementation detail — tests should focus on what the component renders, not on which variable holds the result. The same tests that work for inline conditionals work here:
Element Variables with switch Statements
When the condition is a discrete enum or string constant,
switch
expresses the intent more clearly than a chain of
if/else if
statements. Element variables make
switch
practical — you cannot use
switch
inline in JSX, but you can use it to assign an element variable above the return:
Adding a new alert type requires only a new
case
above. The JSX template is never touched.
Passing Element Variables as Props
Because JSX elements are plain JavaScript values, you can pass an element variable as a prop — especially useful for layout components that accept content slots:
This is the "slots" pattern — a common composition technique in React. Element variables make the call site clean because all conditional decisions are resolved before the
<TwoColumnLayout>
tag.
Lazy Initialisation and Memoised Element Variables
Element variables are recomputed on every render. If constructing the element is expensive (large list, complex calculation), wrap it with
useMemo
to cache the result until its dependencies change:
Only add
useMemo
when profiling shows a real performance problem. Most element variables do not need it — React renders are fast for simple elements.
Anti-Pattern: Too Many Element Variables
Like any pattern, element variables can be overused. Defining a variable for every single condition in a component turns the pre-return section into a maze of its own. Guidelines:
If a condition produces a single short element and is easy to read inline, leave it inline.
If you have more than four or five element variables in one component, consider extracting sub-components — the component is probably doing too much.
Element variables are not a replacement for early returns. When an entire render path is different (loading vs loaded), early returns are simpler.
Summary
An element variable stores a JSX element in a variable, decided before the return statement, then interpolated in the JSX with
Use element variables when inline conditions make the JSX hard to read: complex branches, more than two cases, or the same element appearing multiple times.
Use
const
for single-assignment (two branches),
let
for reassignment (three or more branches).
Element variables can hold
null
— React renders nothing for null, so optional UI slots stay clean.
They are a lightweight alternative to extracting a new component — no new file, no extra props layer needed.
